<!-- Topic 6: Programming Task — Student Roster -->
<!-- Total slides: 3 -->

# Programming Task — Student Roster

---

## Build it: a roster of 5 students {.smaller}

+ Declare a `Student` struct with a `string name` and `int grade`.
+ Allocate a static array of 5 students — **not** a dynamic array.
+ Read each student's name and grade from the user, then print the full roster.

::: notes
Total slides: 3
Give students ~10 minutes. They have all the pieces: struct declaration, static array, for loop, dot notation, cin/cout. The challenge is combining them cleanly. Remind students: static array means Student roster[5]; — no new/delete needed.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Requirements

- Declare `struct Student` with `string name` and `int grade`
- In `main`, declare `Student roster[5]`
- Loop `i = 0` to `4`: read `roster[i].name` and `roster[i].grade`
- Print all 5 records: `"[name]: [grade]"`

::: notes
Keep the output format simple. Students should not need to worry about formatting — just get the data in and back out correctly. If time allows, ask them to also print the highest-grade student after the roster.
:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Solution {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

struct Student {
    string name;
    int    grade;
};

int main() {
    const int SIZE = 5;
    Student   roster[SIZE];

    for (int i = 0; i < SIZE; i++) {
        cout << "Name:  "; cin >> roster[i].name;
        cout << "Grade: "; cin >> roster[i].grade;
    }

    cout << "\n--- Roster ---\n";
    for (int i = 0; i < SIZE; i++)
        cout << roster[i].name << ": " << roster[i].grade << "\n";

    return 0;
}
```

<!-- Slide 3 -->
